fingerprint: SG 'null result' (0.0 μGal) is defined relative to a globe-derived tidal baseline — raw SG data requires subtraction of Newtonian luni-solar tides computed with moon at 384,400 km and sun at 1 AU, not dome's 2,534 km and 5,733 km
globe_value: SG data reduction pipeline: (1) Theoretical Earth tides computed from ephemerides placing the Moon at 384,400 km and Sun at 149.6 million km via Newtonian point-mass tidal potential (Dehant–Defraigne–Wahr tidal parameters). (2) Ocean tidal loading corrections from global ocean tide models (FES2014, GOT4.10c) computed on a spherical Earth mesh. (3) Atmospheric loading corrections using spherical harmonic surface-pressure models. (4) Pole-tide correction using IERS Earth-rotation parameters (rotation of a sphere). (5) Station coordinates in WGS84 (ITRF realization). (6) Eclipse path timing from Besselian elements — spherical-geometry shadow projections.
dome_value: The dome claims the China SG network measured 0.0 μGal during the July 22, 2009 eclipse, citing 'Sun 2010.' The dome interprets this null as confirming an electromagnetic (not gravitational) eclipse mechanism. But the 0.0 μGal is a RESIDUAL — it means the raw SG signal, after subtracting all predicted tidal and loading effects, showed no eclipse-correlated anomaly. Those predictions are entirely globe-derived.
match_precision: The 'null' is only meaningful if the subtracted tidal model is correct to within the SG noise floor (~1 nGal). Globe tidal models achieve this. Under dome parameters (moon at 2,534 km instead of 384,400 km — a factor of 152×), the predicted tidal potential would be completely different in both amplitude and phase, and the residual would NOT be 0.0 μGal.
why_it_matters: The dome's SG null claim has a hidden circular dependency on globe infrastructure at every stage of the measurement pipeline. To isolate a 'null eclipse gravity effect,' the SG operators must first subtract the dominant signals — Earth tides (~100 μGal peak-to-peak), ocean loading (~1–10 μGal depending on station), and atmospheric pressure effects (~0.3 μGal/hPa). All of these corrections are computed using globe geometry: (1) Earth tides use the Moon at 384,400 km in a Keplerian orbit around a spherical Earth — the dome places the Moon at 2,534 km on a circular track above a flat disc, which would predict a completely different tidal potential (single-bulge, not semidiurnal two-bulge, as already established in Kill-Shot #2). The phase, amplitude, and period of the tidal correction depend on lunar distance and orbital mechanics that the dome's geometry cannot reproduce. (2) Ocean loading requires a spherical ocean model — there are no dome-compatible ocean tide models, because the dome's coastlines, ocean depths, and tidal forcing geometry are fundamentally different. (3) Eclipse timing and path geometry — determining WHEN to look for an eclipse signal — requires Besselian elements computed from spherical astronomy. The July 2009 eclipse path across China is a globe-geometry calculation. (4) Even the SG stations' positions (latitude, longitude, height) are defined in WGS84/ITRF, which the dome acknowledges depending on (OPEN-001). If the dome replaced globe tidal corrections with dome-derived predictions (which would require dome-consistent lunar distance, orbit, and geometry — none of which exist), the residual would not be 0.0 μGal. The dome is parasitizing the globe's measurement infrastructure to claim a result that only exists because globe physics was used to define the baseline.
suggested_text: The SG 'null result' (0.0 μGal) is not a raw measurement — it is a residual after subtracting predicted tidal and loading effects computed entirely from globe physics. Earth tides are modeled with the Moon at 384,400 km in a Keplerian orbit around a spherical Earth; ocean loading uses spherical ocean-tide models; eclipse timing uses Besselian elements from spherical astronomy. Under the dome's own parameters (Moon at 2,534 km, flat disc geometry), none of these corrections would be valid — the tidal prediction alone would change by orders of magnitude — and the residual would not be zero. The dome claims a null result whose very definition depends on the globe infrastructure it rejects.
notes: This is the same class of fingerprint as WIN-011 (Mohe spring gravimeter also depends on globe tidal corrections for its -6.5 μGal residual) but the SG case is stronger because SG data reduction is MORE dependent on precise tidal models — the whole point of SG precision is that the tidal signal is modeled and removed to expose sub-μGal residuals. The confidence is 0.80 rather than higher because: (a) we don't have access to the actual Sun 2010 paper to confirm which specific tidal model was used, and (b) the dome could theoretically argue that the null result would persist regardless of tidal model (i.e., the eclipse had no gravity effect at any timescale, so the choice of baseline doesn't matter). However, this defense fails because without the correct tidal baseline, you wouldn't know the result was null — you'd see unexplained variation that might or might not include an eclipse signal. The globe tidal model is what makes the null interpretable.
